text.title: Anti-patterns to avoid
text.summary: Failure modes that destroy the value of multi-agent brainstorm. Each is reject-on-sight: the orchestrator MUST detect and prevent them before launching agents.

rule.statement: MUST NOT launch 10 research agents with identical prompts.
rule.rationale: Identical prompts collapse 10 agents into 1. The recommendations cluster tightly, consensus counting becomes a tautology (10/10 because they all wrote the same thing), and the cost of running 10 agents buys nothing. Distinct personas are non-negotiable.

rule.statement: MUST NOT use generic personas like "senior engineer", "experienced developer", "product expert".
rule.rationale: Generic personas yield generic ideas. The whole point of persona assignment is to bias the agent's search toward ideas only that persona would surface. A specific persona ("Django ORM specialist who has debugged N+1 in production") produces specific ideas; a generic persona produces a bland average.

rule.statement: MUST NOT scale agent count above 12 to "get more coverage".
rule.rationale: Empirical sweet spot is 10-12. Above 12, marginal coverage drops sharply while cost and synthesis-pass complexity rise linearly. More agents do not equal more signal — they equal more noise the synthesis pass has to filter.

rule.statement: MUST NOT let research agents see each other's output during diverge.
rule.rationale: Cross-pollination causes anchoring on whichever agent finishes first. Independence is the entire point of multi-agent diverge — the moment agents see each other, they converge prematurely and the converge phase has nothing left to do.

rule.statement: MUST NOT use generic review prompts like "please review this document" or "evaluate these recommendations".
rule.rationale: Generic prompts yield rubber-stamping: the reviewer reads the recommendations, finds nothing obviously wrong, and replies "LGTM". The output is decorative. Adversarial framing ("attack from vector X", "find what breaks") produces actionable critique.

rule.statement: MUST NOT launch reviewers that share an attack vector or perspective.
rule.rationale: Two reviewers both attacking "feasibility" produce two near-identical critiques and leave seven other vectors uncovered. One vector per reviewer, eight required vectors covered exactly once.

rule.statement: MUST NOT skip re-tiering in Phase 4.
rule.rationale: If reviewer feedback does not change tier assignments, the review phase was decorative and the cost was wasted. Re-tiering is the artifact that makes the review load-bearing.

rule.statement: MUST NOT skip Phase 0 when the user did not explicitly request a brainstorm.
rule.rationale: Auto-routed brainstorm without consent burns the user's quota and trust on output they did not ask for. The orchestrator is allowed to over-trigger; Phase 0 is the safety net. Skipping Phase 0 is a contract violation, not a shortcut.

rule.statement: MUST NOT carry "Run brainstorm" consent across sessions.
rule.rationale: Consent is per-session. A user who approved brainstorm yesterday has not approved today's brainstorm. Re-ask.

rule.statement: MUST NOT present a single option / single recommendation as the brainstorm output.
rule.rationale: The user asked for diverge-converge precisely because they wanted alternatives. A single option discards the entire reason for running 18 agents. Always present a tiered list with explicit alternatives, even when one option is clearly preferred.

rule.statement: MUST NOT omit the "why defer" rationale for backlog items.
rule.rationale: The user needs to see what was rejected and why, not just what survived. Without "why defer", the same idea gets re-litigated next quarter. The rationale is the artifact that prevents that.

rule.statement: MUST NOT skip the self-evaluation pass at the end of Phase 4.
rule.rationale: Self-evaluation (Coverage / Novelty / Actionability / Bias / User-focus) is a cheap groupthink detector. If novelty is low or bias is "infrastructure-only", the persona assignment failed and the next brainstorm should adjust. Skipping the eval blinds the orchestrator to its own failure modes.
